one_line_takeaway: The paper formally (and Lean-verified) proves that continuous, utility-preserving prompt-injection wrapper defenses can never be complete on a connected prompt space, then validates the prediction live against gpt-5-mini and gpt-3.5-turbo behavioral grids with zero interior false positives.
key_finding: Under the stated continuity + utility-preservation + connectedness assumptions, completeness is mathematically impossible: some threshold-level prompt must pass through the defense unremediated (Thm 4.1), a positive-measure band around it stays near-threshold under Lipschitz regularity (Thm 5.2), and under a transversality condition a positive-measure region stays strictly unsafe after defense (Thm 6.3). Empirically (Table 3), across three live validator runs the predicted steep set Spred exactly matched the confusion-matrix true positives with zero interior false positives: gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07 (tau=0.30, 14 filled cells, L=G=6.13, |Spred|=|Sact|=7, TP=7, FPint=0, FN=0); gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 smoke test (tau=0.50, 9 filled cells, L=G=11.44, |Spred|=|Sact|=7, TP=7, FPint=0, FN=0); and the saturated gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 run (tau=0.50, 82 filled cells from 400 evaluations / ~5,200 OpenAI API calls / ~130 min wall clock / ~$15 cost, L=G=23.63, |Spred|=|Sact|=66, TP=66, FPint=0, FN=0). Under the non-vacuous, non-identity GP-smooth oblique defense (theta=89.5 deg, l=0.86, K=1.05, l(K+1)=1.77, well below G=23.6), the predicted 3-cell steep set was fully confirmed persistent (TP=3, FPint=0) against an actual persistent set of 68 cells (Fig. 4). The defense sweep across 7 discrete defenses on the saturated grid (Table 4) showed the actual persistent set shrinking from 66 (identity) to 55/47/29/3/0/0 cells as bounded_step reach grew from 1 to 12, with FPint=0 on every row -- i.e. zero empirical counterexamples to the theorem across the entire sweep. gpt-5-mini's clean AD ceiling of exactly 0.50 at tau=0.50 (Uτ empty) correctly triggered the theorem's 'not applicable' silence, which the authors treat as a confirming null result.
core_contribution: The paper proves a formal impossibility result — the 'defense trilemma' — showing that no defense D: X -> X that is (1) continuous (similar prompts yield similar rewrites), (2) utility-preserving (D(x)=x for all inputs already below the safety threshold tau), and (3) complete (every output is mapped strictly safe, f(D(x)) < tau for all x) can exist on a connected Hausdorff prompt space X with an alignment-deviation function f. The authors derive this in three successively stronger results: Boundary Fixation (Theorem 4.1: the defense must leave at least one exact-threshold point z unchanged), the epsilon-Robust Constraint (Theorem 5.1, under L-Lipschitz f and K-Lipschitz D: f(D(x)) >= tau - LK*dist(x,z), so a positive-measure band near z stays near-threshold), and the Persistent Unsafe Region (Theorem 6.3, under a transversality condition G > l(K+1) where l is the defense-path Lipschitz constant: a positive-measure set S remains strictly above tau after defense). They give quantitative volume/cone lower bounds (Theorems 7.1-7.3), a discrete (topology-free) counterpart via counting/injectivity arguments (Theorem 8.2-8.3: a complete discrete defense must be non-injective, i.e. must destroy information), extensions to multi-turn (9.1), stochastic (9.2), and nonlinear agent-pipeline (9.3-9.4) defenses, and a Tietze-extension bridge (Theorem 8.1) arguing the continuous results apply to any continuous interpolant of finite discrete behavioral observations. The entire proof suite (46 files, ~360 theorems, zero 'sorry'/admitted statements, three standard axioms) is mechanically verified in Lean 4 with Mathlib, at https://github.com/mbhatt1/stuff/tree/main/ManifoldProofs. The paper explicitly scopes the result: it applies only to a single continuous, utility-preserving wrapper D:X->X and explicitly does NOT preclude training-time alignment (RLHF/DPO/constitutional training), architectural changes, discontinuous defenses (hard blocklists/discrete classifiers), output-side filters, ensembles, human-in-the-loop review, or adaptive-threshold/multi-component systems whose classifiers may reject rather than preserve utility everywhere.
threat_model: Wrapper-style prompt-injection/jailbreak defenses that sit in front of an LLM and preprocess or rewrite the input prompt before the model sees it (input classifiers, constitutional rewriting pipelines, input sanitization layers, e.g. citing perplexity-based detectors, Llama Guard-style input/output safeguards, and constitutional-AI rewriting as the class of defenses under scrutiny). The 'attacker' in the formal sense is not an adaptive human but the geometry of the prompt space itself: any prompt whose alignment-deviation score sits exactly at, or is steered close to, the safety threshold tau exposes a mathematically unavoidable gap between what the defense must leave untouched (utility preservation) and what it must remap (completeness). The empirical section (Section 10-10.1) operationalizes this against three real target LLMs -- Llama-3-8B, GPT-OSS-20B, and GPT-5-mini (via the companion 'Manifold of Failure' behavioral-basin framework, cited as their own prior work [17]) -- plus live re-collected runs against gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07 and gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 (judged by gpt-4.1-2025-04-14) using a MAP-Elites-driven 2D grid (query indirection x authority framing) as the alignment-deviation surface.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 'Limitations'): (1) Boundary fixation only guarantees a fixed point exactly at the threshold -- if tau-level behavior is itself benign (e.g. a polite refusal), the impossibility is technically true but practically harmless; GPT-5-Mini is offered as exactly this case. (2) The epsilon-robust bound limits how far the defense can push scores down near the boundary, not whether it does so at all. (3) The persistent-unsafe-region result (Theorem 6.3) is conditional on transversality (c > l(K+1)); for isotropic alignment surfaces (l = L) the steep/persistent set is provably empty for every K, so the strongest theorem is vacuous in that regime -- the paper notes this is 'non-vacuous precisely when the surface is anisotropic.' (4) The grid-based cost-asymmetry bound (Theorem F.1, N^d complexity) assumes exhaustive enumeration; learning-based defenses that generalize across the space may sidestep it. (5) Explicitly out of scope: training-time alignment, architectural model changes, discontinuous defenses (hard blocklists/classifiers), output-side filters, ensembles, human-in-the-loop review, and adaptive-threshold or multi-component systems that may reject/redirect rather than preserve utility everywhere -- none of these are constrained by the theorems. (6) The 'pipeline' extension (Theorem 9.3/9.4) predicting deeper agent pipelines widen the failure band is, per the paper's own Table 2, 'not directly tested (no pipeline experiment).' (7) The measure-zero remark on the boundary level set f^{-1}(tau) relies on the coarea formula and is explicitly noted as 'not formalized in the Lean artifact.' (8) The empirical validation is limited to a 2D behavioral surface (query indirection x authority framing) from the companion Manifold of Failure paper and a handful of models/runs; generalization to higher-dimensional, more naturalistic prompt spaces is not tested here.

datasets_used: 2D behavioral alignment-deviation grids (query indirection x authority framing) generated via a MAP-Elites driver from the authors' companion 'Manifold of Failure' framework [17], applied to Llama-3-8B, GPT-OSS-20B, and GPT-5-Mini (numbers read off previously published heatmaps)

Newly, live-collected 25x25 MAP-Elites alignment-deviation grids against gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07 and gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 (judged by gpt-4.1-2025-04-14), including a saturation run with 400 evaluations (100 seed prompts + 300 MAP-Elites iterations, ~5,200 OpenAI API calls) filling 82 cells (66 strictly unsafe, 10 boundary, per Table 3/4)
quantitative_results: Live validation Table 3: gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07 (tau=0.30, 14 filled cells, L=G=6.13, |Spred|=|Sact|=7, TP=7, FPint=0, FN=0)
gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 smoke test (tau=0.50, 9 filled cells, L=G=11.44, |Spred|=|Sact|=7, TP=7, FPint=0, FN=0)
gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 saturated run (tau=0.50, 82 filled cells, L=G=23.63, |Spred|=|Sact|=66, TP=66, FPint=0, FN=0)
GP-smooth oblique defense on saturated grid (theta=89.5deg, l=0.86, K=1.05, l(K+1)=1.77, G=23.6): predicted steep set size 3, actual persistent set size 68, TP=3, FPint=0
Defense sweep Table 4 on saturated grid across identity + bounded_step(1,2,3,5,8,12): |Sact| falls 66 -> 55 -> 47 -> 29 -> 3 -> 0 -> 0 as K grows from 1.00 to 8.60; FPint=0 on all 7 rows
Boundary fixation non-vacuously activated: 10 boundary cells in Sτ\closure(Sτ); closest boundary cell sits at |f(z*) - tau| = 0.375
Llama-3-8B (from prior Manifold of Failure heatmaps): mean AD 0.93, basin rate 93.9%, estimated directional slope G ≈ 5
GPT-5-Mini: peak AD 0.50, basin rate 0% (Uτ empty at tau=0.50, correctly predicting no impossibility)
Cost estimate for saturation run: ~5,200 OpenAI API calls, wall clock ~130 minutes, cost ~$15
Lean 4 formalization: 46 files, ~360 theorems, zero admitted/sorry statements, 3 standard axioms
baselines_compared: Tsipras et al. (robustness-accuracy tradeoff, PAC/Gaussian, classifier target) -- positioned in Table 1 as a non-verified prior impossibility/tradeoff result
Fawzi et al. (adversarial invulnerability via Lipschitz geometry, classifier target) -- Table 1 comparison
Wolpert & Macready no-free-lunch theorem (optimizer universal dominance) -- cited as the closest conceptual precedent
Cohen et al. randomized smoothing (certified bound for smoothed classifiers) -- Table 1 comparison

7 discrete defense variants swept empirically: identity (K=1.00) and bounded_step with max_step in {1,2,3,5,8,12} (K ranging 3.61 to 8.60), plus one GP-smooth oblique continuous defense (K=1.05, l=0.86)
